comments:
Summary:
This paper proposes a nested-attention hybrid neural sequence-to-sequence model for grammatical error correction (GEC). The architecture augments a word-level encoder-decoder backbone with character-level encoders and decoders, and introduces a second (nested) character-level attention that is used when both the source and target tokens are out-of-vocabulary. The model is evaluated on CoNLL-13/14 data and shows substantial improvements over a strong word-level NMT baseline and a single-level hybrid model; further gains are obtained by reranking with a web-scale 5-gram LM. The authors analyze the contribution of nested attention, showing > gains on edits involving small orthographic changes.

Strengths:
- Clear, well-motivated problem statement: GEC requires both global (word-level) and local (character-level) reasoning. The nested-attention design addresses this need naturally.
- Sound, end-to-end architecture that builds on prior hybrid work (Luong & Manning) but extends it meaningfully by adding character-level attention tied to word-level alignment.
- Thorough experimental evaluation: controlled baseline reimplementation, comparison to single-level hybrid, integration with large LM, and focused analysis on OOV vs non-OOV and small vs large orthographic edits.
- Empirical gains are convincing: nested attention improves correction of orthographically similar rare-word errors, which is an important class of GEC mistakes.

Weaknesses / Points for improvement:
- Comparison to absolute state-of-the-art (phrase-based MT approaches that report higher F0.5) is discussed but not directly evaluated with the same training/decoding setup. A direct head-to-head or hybrid integration discussion would strengthen claims about final competitive performance.
- The choice to apply character-level attention only when source tokens are OOV is reasonable for controlled comparison, but it limits potential gains on known-word edits; some ablation applying nested attention to all tokens would be informative.
- Hyperparameter choices (α, β, vocabulary selection rule) are described, but more detail on sensitivity and ablation (e.g., effect of character-loss weights, beam sizes for character decoder) would aid reproducibility and understanding of robustness.
- Training cost is substantial (several days on a high-end GPU); discussion of runtime/decoding complexity and practical deployment considerations is limited.
- While several qualitative examples are given, a larger set of illustrative error corrections (including failure cases) would help readers understand model behavior.

Overall assessment:
This is a solid and well-executed paper that contributes a clear architectural innovation targeted to the needs of GEC. It demonstrates meaningful empirical improvements and provides useful analyses that explain where gains come from. The work is incremental relative to the broad NMT literature (building on hybrid models), but the nested attention idea and its focused application to GEC are novel and practically useful. I recommend acceptance and would encourage the authors to expand comparisons to other subword approaches (BPE/word-piece, full char-level models) and to report more ablation and practical details to aid reproducibility.
